course_description:
This course explores the core principles and practices of food and beverage management systems. It examines the operational efficiency of food and beverage production and service facilities in the international hospitality industry.

This course is designed to equip students seeking careers in food and beverage with concepts and tools used in its management. Further, HOSP2005 will also appeal to those students seeking careers in the broader hospitality industry, as it underlines the strategic importance of the department within organisations. This course cannot compensate for the technical knowledge and skills acquired ‘on-the-job’ but instead focuses on the managerial knowledge and skills required to understand and subsequently manage operations.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

Secure assessment modes are incorporated into the assessment design. A team presentation is embedded to reflect the teamwork-intensive nature of professional practice in the hospitality industry.

Sustainable Development Goals - UQ Business School is a proud supporter and Advanced Signatory of the United Nations Principles for Responsible Management Education (UN PRME) . As part of the largest global collaboration between business schools and the UN, the school emphasises its role in empowering students to drive societal transformation through the Sustainable Development Goals . The SDGs highlight that a thriving economy relies on a healthy environment, aiming to balance economic growth, social well-being, and environmental protection for a sustainable future.

course_aims:
This course’s overall aim is to introduce students to the core principles and practices of food and beverage management systems which optimise the managerial and operational efficiency of food and beverage production and service facilities, especially as they apply in the international hotel industry.

The three principal aims of the course are to:

learning_outcomes.description: Analyse and discuss the diverse contexts in which food and beverage production and service is delivered, managed and marketed.
Explain the processes and methods of food and beverage production and service using the systems approach.
Evaluate the concept of food and beverage profitability, and become conversant with tools for stock and cost control and revenue maximisation.
Collaboratively develop strategies for the continuous improvement of food and beverage production, service and management within evolving legal and regulatory frameworks.
Highlight the difference in organisation and operation of other major segments of the food and beverage service industry.

assessment_details.title: Reflective Essay
assessment_details.task_description: Each individual should prepare a maximum 3,000-word reflective essay. In this reflective essay, you are asked to identify critical learning event(s) that actually happened during your experience at a restaurant. You then analyse the most significant of these events using a four-stage analysis. The reading list below will provide you with various theoretical foundations. You are required to choose THREE (3) articles from the reading lists presented below to prepare a reflective essay. You are also required to cite the articles of your choice (APA style) in your essay. In this reflective essay, you are expected to analyse the event(s) in the following four-stage: Situation: What actually happened to you? Affect: What was its impact on you as a customer? Interpretation: Why do you think the restaurant acted that way? Which article provides the theoretical underpinnings of the event/s? Suggestion: What would you recommend to the restaurant to improve the situation? 1. Situation Choose one (or more if you want) critical learning event that seemed the most significant during your past experience at a restaurant. It does not have to be something positive; we often learn more from negative experiences. At this stage, write what actually happened. Events or incidents will vary in complexity and depth. 2. Affect Think about the event and how it affected you as a customer. This could be positive or negative. You may have felt frustrated, confused, angry, elated, relieved or something else. Elaborate on why you felt so. 3. Interpretation Think about and then explain in what ways the event(s) either confirm/s or contradict/s the theoretical underpinnings of the article(s) you chose. This is where a theory meets reality. 4. Suggestion There is no point in learning something if you do not apply it in the future. So what suggestion/s do you make to help the restaurant and/or theory in the future? What can the restaurant do better in the future? You can choose an additional article from the list to support your suggestion. READING LIST FOR THIS ASSIGNMENT (Pick THREE) Berenguer, G., Gil, I., & Ruiz, M. E. (2009). Do upscale restaurant owners use wine lists as a differentiation strategy. International Journal of Hospitality Management , 28(1), 86-95. Kim, J.-Y., Natter, M., & Spann, M. (2009). Pay what you want: A new participative pricing mechanism. Journal of Marketing , 73(1), 44-58. Hwang, J., & Lambert, C. U. (2009). The Use of Acceptable Customer Waiting Times for Capacity Management in a Multistage Restaurant. Journal of Hospitality & Tourism Research , 33(4), 547-561. Kimes, S. E. (2011). The future of distribution management in the restaurant industry. Journal of Revenue and Pricing Management , 10(2), 189-194. Ma, E., Qu, H., & Njite, D. (2011). U.S. customer perceptions toward Chinese restaurant service quality: An importance and performance approach. Journal of Foodservice Business Research , 14(3), 290-308. Yang, S. S. (2012). Eye movements on restaurant menus: A revisitation on gaze motion and consumer scanpaths. International Journal of Hospitality Management , 31(3), 1021-1029. Heung, V., & Gu, T. (2012). Influence of restaurant atmospherics on patron satisfaction and behavioral intentions. International Journal of Hospitality Management , 31(4), 1167-1177. Guéguen, N., & Jacob, C. (2012). Lipstick and tipping behavior: When red lipstick enhances waitresses tips. International Journal of Hospitality Management , 31(4), 1333-1335. Bruwer, J., & Rawbone-Viljoen, C. (2013). BYOB as a risk-reduction strategy (RRS) for wine consumers in the Australian on-premise foodservice sector: Exploratory insights. International Journal of Hospitality Management , 32, 21-30. Heo, C. Y., Lee, S., Mattila, A., & Hu, C. (2013). Restaurant revenue management: Do perceived capacity scarcity and price differences matter? International Journal of Hospitality Management , 35, 316-326. Wansink, B., & Love, K. (2014). Slim by design: Menu strategies for promoting high-margin, healthy foods. International Journal of Hospitality Management , 42, 137-143. Thompson, G. M. (2015). An evaluation of rules for assigning tables to walk-in parties in restaurants. Cornell Hospitality Quarterly , 56(1), 91-105. Yepes, M. F. (2015). Mobile tablet menus: attractiveness and impact of nutrition labeling formats on millennials’ food choices. Cornell Hospitality Quarterly , 56(1), 58-67. Magnini, V. P ., & Kim, S. (2016). The influences of restaurant menu font style, background color, and physical weight on consumers’ perceptions. International Journal of Hospitality Management , 53, 42-48. Han, S. J., Bonn, M. A., & Cho, M. (2016). The relationship between customer incivility, restaurant frontline service employee burnout and turnover intention. International Journal of Hospitality Management , 52, 97-106. Lee, A., & Kim, M-G. (2019). Effective electronic menu presentation: From the cognitive style and mental imagery perspectives. International Journal of Hospitality Management , 52, 97-106. https://doi.org/10/1016/j.ijhm.2019.102377. Lu, L., Zhang, P., & Zhang, T. (2021). Leveraging “human-likeness” of robotic service at restaurants. International Journal of Hospitality Management , 94 , 102823. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhm.2020.102823 EARLY CHECK (Week 3) Before submitting your completed reflective essay, you are required to participate in a brief oral check during your tutorial in Week 3 (if your oral check is not completed during your tutorial, it will continue during the lecture in Week 3). This is a formative (not graded) activity designed to support your progress and ensure you are on the right track before final submission. For this early check, you should select ONE article from your chosen three and be prepared to briefly present or discuss how the theory in that article connects to a real experience you had at a restaurant. You do not need a polished presentation — this is an early, exploratory conversation to help you develop your thinking. Your tutor will provide you with immediate verbal feedback that is intended to feed forward into the completion of your full reflective essay, due in Week 6. ORAL CHECK-IN (Week 7) To ensure academic integrity, an oral check-in has been implemented for this assignment in Week 7. Following the submission of your reflective essay, you are required to participate in an oral check-in during your tutorial in Week 7 (if your oral check-in is not completed during your tutorial, it will continue during the lecture (or online if necessary) in Week 7). In this session, your tutor will ask questions about your chosen articles, the critical event(s) you described, or the reasoning behind your analysis and suggestions. This check-in is designed to verify that the submitted work is genuinely your own and that you have a sound understanding of the material you engaged with. You should therefore be thoroughly familiar with every part of your essay before attending this session. The oral check-in is assessed against specific criteria of the assessment rubric. Students who make no attempt to complete the oral check-in will receive a 0 for these criteria. GUIDELINES The restaurant experience that you will reflect on must be one that you had from an actual restaurant visit. AI Statement: Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, you will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of your written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Each student needs to submit the document file to Blackboard.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Business Advice on Restaurant Management
assessment_details.other_conditions: Peer assessment factor. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Working in the same tutorial team, your team is required to create a 20-minute presentation on restaurant operation and management. In this presentation, your team is required to provide business advice to a restaurant (see Task 1 below to know how to select a restaurant) by addressing the following four (4) questions : What areas or aspects of the ordering system is your team most satisfied with? What areas or aspects of the ordering system is your team least satisfied with? How do you think the ordering system affects the restaurant operation and management? What business advice would your team give to the restaurant owner? To complete this assignment, the following tasks are suggested: Task 1. Restaurant choice To complete this assignment, your team must purposely visit and dine at a restaurant that has implemented a digital ordering system. Orders must be placed using at least one of the following methods: tablets installed on tables, mobile devices via QR codes provided at tables, or self-service kiosks within the restaurant. Where available, your team may also place orders directly with service staff for comparison purposes. Ordering through third-party delivery platforms (e.g., Uber Eats or DoorDash) is not permitted. At least one selfie taken in the restaurant must be included. All members should appear together where possible; otherwise, each member must appear in at least one selfie. The restaurant selected for this assignment must be different from the one used in Assignment 1. Fast-food chains (e.g., McDonald's and Hungry Jack's) should be avoided where possible. Task 2. Description of the restaurant Reflecting the topics covered throughout the semester , your team is expected to describe your chosen restaurant and its management/operation in detail (e.g., current clientele, concept, F&B menu type, service style, marketing and so forth). Where appropriate, make explicit reference to relevant lecture topic(s) (e.g., service style from Lecture Topic X). Task 3. Analysis of digital ordering experience Provide a detailed description of the restaurant's ordering system. Your discussion should include, but is not limited to, the design and layout of the interface (e.g., screen structure, menu organisation, visual clarity), the ordering process (e.g., number of steps, navigation flow), and the overall ease or difficulty of use. You should also reflect on usability aspects, including features that support or hinder the dining experience from a customer perspective. Where relevant, comment on how the ordering system integrates with the restaurant's service style and operations. Task 4. Performance evaluation and analysis Using systems approach , assess the overall performance of the restaurant based on your own experience, with particular attention to how the ordering experience influences restaurant operations and management. The DINESERV template may be used to support this assessment. This task will help you to answer the third question. Task 5. Business advice To answer the last question, your team is required to provide feasible recommendations to the restaurant owner. Your business advice should reflect what you have learnt throughout the semester and be realistic and actionable. "Feasible" means that your advice is financially and practically achievable. Task 6. Presentation Based on your findings from Tasks 1 to 5, prepare a 20-minute PowerPoint Slide show. In the presentation slides, your team are expected to answer the four questions using evidence-based analysis. FORMAT: This assignment does not require a live presentation. Instead, your team will create a recorded PowerPoint slideshow (maximum 20 minutes) with video on , as if presenting to a live audience. If you are unfamiliar with recording a PowerPoint slideshow with narration, click HERE for guidance. All team members must appear in the video presentation. When presenting, each member is required to record both their physical appearance and voice (narration). Each team needs to submit one file to the course Blackboard site. A team representative should be appointed to submit the file on behalf of all members. Only one submission per team is required; individual submissions are not necessary. The MS PowerPoint file format (PPTX) is strongly recommended for the video presentation. If alternative software is used, it is the team's responsibility to ensure successful submission and accessibility for the teaching team without requiring additional software. Uploading a video file (e.g., MP4) directly to Blackboard is likely to result in submission failure. Please note that the file submission will take time, particularly when submitting close to the deadline. Without approved extension, any file submission after the due date will incur late submission penalty. Plan your submission accordingly. In some cases, large PPTX files may not be playable on the web. This is acceptable. The teaching team will download and review submitted files offline. As long as your submission record is visible in Blackboard, your submission will be considered valid. Please ensure that all your team members retain the submission record . The teaching team will mark the file submitted to the system, whether it is an incomplete or incorrect file. Peer Evaluation A peer evaluation is required for this team assignment. You will be asked to evaluate each student's contribution to the assessment task through peer ratings through BuddyCheck. Final individual grades will be calculated by weighting the team mark according to the peer evaluation scores. AI Statement: Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_activities.topic: Topic 1: The F&B Operations and Management Course overview & F&B management overview Learning outcomes: L01, L02
F&B Industry and Sectors Understand and familiarise with the F&B industry and sectors; Discuss entrepreneurship in the F&B industry Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05
Topic 2: Concept & Product Development Systems approach to the development of a consumer product relationship Learning outcomes: L01, L02
Early Check A brief oral check to support your progress and ensure you are on the right track before final submission. Please check Assignment 1 on ECP for detail. Learning outcomes: L01
Topic 3: The Menu (Part 1) Considerations of menu planning Learning outcomes: L01, L03
Exploring new concept: Ghost kitchen Search the concept online and discuss how this new concept emerged.
Topic 4: The Menu (Part 2) Different approaches to menu pricing
Menu categories & description Develop a menu based on recipe
Topic 5: The Menu (Part 3) Two menu analysis methods
Base selling price Set a base-selling price based on recipe Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
Topic 6: Food Production and Service Purchasing function and service methods
In-Semester Break
Oral Check-in A five-minute oral check-in (Q&A) will be conducted for each student.
Oral Check-in A five-minute oral check-in (Q&A) will be conducted for each student.
Menu Engineering analysis Web-based exercise for Menu Engineering Analysis
Topic 7: Beverage Production and Service Beverage list and control of beverage stocks
Measuring F&B Production & Service Discuss production and services with examples and discuss Dineserv and service quality (web-based).
Topic 8: F&B Human Resources Basic steps in the HR function within F&B management
Ethics in F&B Operation Exercise with Business ethics
Topic 9: F&B Marketing Essentials of F&B marketing Learning outcomes: L01, L02
Financial statement exercise Web-based practice of financial statement reading and interpretation
Topic 10: F&B Financial Management I Basics of accounting principles
Break-even analysis exercise Web-based practice of two types of Break-even analysis
Topic 11: F&B Financial Management II Break-even analysis and budget
SWOT analysis and Review SWOT analysis exercise & Review of course. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05
Topic 12: Appraising F&B Performance Basis for F&B performance appraisal

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library .
